rows.title: EDPB Adopts Scientific Research Guidelines and Approves Europrivacy Certification at 118th Plenary
rows.summary:
The European Data Protection Board adopted guidelines on processing personal data for scientific research purposes, established a sprint team to finalise anonymisation guidelines, and approved Europrivacy certification criteria as a European data protection seal at its 118th plenary session on 15 and 16 April 2026.

On 15 and 16 April 2026, the European Data Protection Board (EDPB) held its 118th plenary session, during which it adopted Guidelines 1/2026 on the processing of personal data for scientific research purposes under the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR). The EDPB opened the guidelines for public consultation until 25 June 2026.

The EDPB finds that determining whether processing qualifies as scientific research requires consideration of six key factors: a methodical and systematic approach; adherence to ethical standards; verifiability and transparency; autonomy and independence; the objectives of the research; and the potential to develop or apply existing scientific knowledge in a novel way. The EDPB determines that where research activities satisfy these factors, they may be recognised as scientific research for GDPR purposes, and controllers must justify why activities that do not satisfy them merit that classification.

The EDPB states that processing for scientific research purposes is considered compatible with the original purpose of data collection under Article 5(1)(b) GDPR, meaning controllers are not required to conduct a compatibility test. However, the EDPB notes that controllers must ensure the legal basis for the original processing is also appropriate for the subsequent research processing. The EDPB clarifies that broad consent may be relied upon where research purposes are not fully known at data collection, provided researchers respect applicable ethical standards and implement additional safeguards. The EDPB additionally clarifies the application of data subject rights, including the right to erasure and the right to object, and notes that restrictions may apply where processing is necessary for a task carried out in the public interest.

The EDPB established a dedicated sprint team to complete work on forthcoming anonymisation guidelines by summer 2026, and adopted opinions approving Europrivacy certification criteria as a European data protection seal, one of which the EDPB determines may be used as a data transfer mechanism under the GDPR.

rows.title: Polish Supreme Administrative Court Upholds UODO Order Against Debt Funds for Unlawful Processing of Heir's Personal Data
rows.summary:
Poland's Supreme Administrative Court has dismissed the cassation appeal of BEST Towarzystwo Funduszy Inwestycyjnych S.A., confirming that debt collection entities lacked a legal basis under GDPR to process the personal data of a deceased debtor's potential heir in order to pursue repayment of the deceased's obligations.

On 8 April 2026, the Naczelny Sąd Administracyjny (Supreme Administrative Court) dismissed the cassation appeal brought by BEST Towarzystwo Funduszy Inwestycyjnych S.A. against the judgment of the Provincial Administrative Court in Warsaw of 17 November 2022, which had itself upheld the 2021 decision of the President of the Urząd Ochrony Danych Osobowych (UODO), Poland's data protection authority. No financial penalty was reported in the underlying decision; the authority's order required deletion of the applicant's personal data.

The President of UODO determined that BEST S.A. and BEST III NSFIZ, represented by BEST Towarzystwo Funduszy Inwestycyjnych, had no legal basis permitting them to process the complainant's personal data for the purpose of enforcing a claim that originated with a deceased individual. The authority concluded that the mere death of a debtor does not render that person's descendants automatically liable for the debtor's obligations, and that the processing of heir data on that assumption therefore lacked a lawful basis within the meaning of Article 6 GDPR. The Supreme Administrative Court confirmed this reasoning in full.

The President of UODO further found that neither entity was entitled to determine independently the circle of heirs to a deceased person's estate. The authority stated that establishing who has acquired an inheritance, and to what extent, is a matter reserved for common court proceedings specifically convened to determine the acquisition of inheritance. Only a formal legal determination of that acquisition would, in the President of UODO's view, establish with sufficient certainty the identity of those liable for the testator's debts. The authority also noted a broader pattern in complaints received by UODO, whereby creditors direct repayment demands at individuals who may not have accepted the inheritance or who may have expressly rejected it, in circumstances where no lawful ground for processing their personal data exists.

rows.title: CERT Polska Annual Report 2025: Incident Handling, APT Monitoring and Fraud Campaigns Across Polish Cyberspace
rows.summary:
CERT Polska, operating within the structures of NASK National Research Institute and executing tasks of CSIRT NASK under the Act on National Cybersecurity System, published its Annual Report 2025 on 3 April 2026, setting out the team's incident-handling activities, threat intelligence findings and operational projects across the preceding year.

On 3 April 2026, CERT Polska released its Annual Report 2025, covering the full calendar year 2025, which also marked the team's 30th anniversary since its establishment in 1996. The report is published under a Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International Licence by the National Research Institute NASK, Warsaw.

The report details CERT Polska's statutory responsibilities under Article 26 of the Act of 5 July 2018 on the National Cybersecurity System, which CERT Polska finds include monitoring cybersecurity threats and incidents at the national level, responding to reported incidents, coordinating incident handling, performing advanced malware and vulnerability analyses, developing detection tools, and conducting awareness-raising activities. CERT Polska states it coordinates incidents reported by a range of entities including public finance sector units, research institutes, digital service providers and commercial companies performing public service tasks.

With respect to threats, CERT Polska reports on widespread fraud campaigns, mobile malware, ransomware attacks and the observed activities of advanced persistent threat groups. CERT Polska also reports on key vulnerabilities and data leaks identified during the year. Operationally, the report covers the Warning List, SMS fraud countermeasures, coordinated vulnerability disclosure processes under the team's #BezpiecznyPrzemysł programme, web application audits, and security analyses of public sector mobile applications. The report further describes CERT Polska's contribution to Poland's Presidency of the Council of the European Union and participation in international exercises including Locked Shields 2025, alongside ongoing development of its own tools and services, among them Artemis, MWDB, n6, AIPITCH, PERUN, FETTA, DNS4EU and the moje.cert.pl platform.

rows.title: EDPB and EDPS call for data protection safeguards in European Biotech Act
rows.summary:
The European Data Protection Board and European Data Protection Supervisor have adopted a joint opinion on the proposed European Biotech Act, calling for specific protective measures for health and genetic data in clinical trials whilst supporting the harmonisation objective.

On 10 March 2026, the European Data Protection Board (EDPB) and the European Data Protection Supervisor (EDPS) adopted Joint Opinion 3/2026. The opinion responds to the European Commission's proposal for a European Biotech Act, which aims to streamline the regulatory framework and update rules on clinical trials.

The EDPB and EDPS find that the proposal's goal of addressing fragmentation in clinical trials regulation is supported. The two bodies welcome the aim of establishing a unified legal basis for processing personal data by sponsors and investigators, which the EDPB and EDPS find would improve legal clarity across the EU. However, the EDPB and EDPS determine that the sensitivity of health data and genetic data in clinical trial contexts requires a high standard of protection and that proposed simplifications must not reduce protection afforded to trial participants.

The joint opinion sets out several recommendations. The EDPB and EDPS find that the proposal is required to clarify whether entities involved in funding and conducting clinical trials act as independent or joint controllers to ensure clear allocation of responsibilities. The EDPB and EDPS find that the mandatory 25-year data retention period is required to refer explicitly to main clinical trial documentation only, not all personal data processed during a trial. The EDPB and EDPS find that the European Biotech Act is required to specify clearly the purposes and safeguards where data are reused for other clinical or scientific research. The EDPB and EDPS find that pseudonymisation is required whenever direct identification of participants is unnecessary, and that regulatory sandboxes are required to provide an explicit legal basis for personal data processing, including derogations for special category data.
